ground_truth.title: Real Estate Investing for Beginners: 5 Skills of Successful Investors
ground_truth.author: Valerie Kirk
ground_truth.main_content:
Valued at more than $230 trillion, real estate investments are the world’s largest asset class and one of the most entrepreneurial fields, drawing people in with the promise of predictable, long-term passive income, positive returns on investments, and favorable tax advantages.

Because real estate is often considered a low-risk investment, many are quick to jump in with expectations of turning a quick profit. Popular television shows on buying investment property and flipping houses feed into the idea that investing in real estate is fun and easy.

However, not all real estate investments are low risk. Some require specialized real estate skills and many require patience and time to realize a positive return on investment.

If you’re considering becoming a real estate investor and entrepreneur, this guide to the real estate industry offers an overview of real estate investing for beginners and the five skills needed for successful real estate investment careers.  

Types of Real Estate

Not all real estate is the same — properties are often zoned for specific uses. Before you become an investor, it is important  to understand the different types of real estate and typical zoning regulations to guide you on what to include in your investment portfolio.

Residential

Residential real estate is property that has been specifically zoned for living in. This includes single- and multi-family homes and apartment buildings.

Commercial Property

Commercial property is real estate that has been zoned for businesses, typically industrial or retail. This could include shopping malls, office buildings, warehouses, and manufacturing sites. 

Land

Land refers to the ground surface and extends both below the earth’s surface and up into higher airspace. It encompasses any structures, water, plants, or minerals located on the land. Land can also be zoned as residential, commercial, industrial, agricultural, or other specific local zoning designations. Developers may petition local governments to change the zoning laws for land that they own (or want to buy).

Potential Career Paths for Aspiring Real Estate Professionals

There are so many career paths in real estate, but before you jump in, it’s important to understand that there is a difference between a real estate agent and a real estate investor. Real estate agents are licensed professionals who help people buy and sell property. Real estate investors are entrepreneurs who buy property to generate income or profit. Many real estate investors are also licensed real estate agents, but it is not a requirement.

Real Estate Investor

Real estate investors buy property to generate income or profit. They may buy and hold property to generate rental income, or they may buy property, renovate it, and sell it for a profit (also known as flipping). Real estate investors may also invest in real estate investment trusts (REITs), which are companies that own, operate, or finance income-generating real estate.

Real Estate Developer

Real estate developers buy land and build new structures or renovate existing ones. They may also change the zoning of land to increase its value. Real estate developers often work with architects, contractors, and city planners to bring their vision to life.

Real Estate Agent

Real estate agents are licensed professionals who help people buy and sell property. They may work with buyers, sellers, or both. Real estate agents must complete pre-licensing education and pass a state exam to become licensed. They must also complete continuing education courses to maintain their license.

Real Estate Broker

Real estate brokers are licensed professionals who have additional education and experience beyond that of a real estate agent. They can work independently or hire agents to work for them. Real estate brokers may also own their own real estate brokerage.

Real Estate Appraiser

Real estate appraisers determine the value of property. They may work for banks, mortgage companies, or government agencies. Real estate appraisers must complete pre-licensing education and pass a state exam to become licensed. They must also complete continuing education courses to maintain their license.

Real Estate Property Manager

Real estate property managers oversee the day-to-day operations of rental properties. They may collect rent, handle maintenance requests, and screen tenants. Real estate property managers may work for property management companies or be self-employed.

5 Skills of Successful Real Estate Investors

Successful real estate investors have a combination of hard and soft skills. Here are five skills that are essential for success in real estate investing.

1. Financial Analysis

Real estate investors must be able to analyze the financial aspects of a property to determine if it is a good investment. This includes calculating the potential return on investment (ROI), cash flow, and cap rate. Real estate investors must also be able to analyze market trends and economic indicators to make informed investment decisions.

2. Negotiation

Real estate investors must be able to negotiate with sellers, buyers, contractors, and other professionals. Negotiation skills are essential for getting the best price on a property, securing favorable financing terms, and hiring contractors at a reasonable cost.

3. Market Research

Real estate investors must be able to research the local real estate market to identify trends and opportunities. This includes analyzing comparable sales, rental rates, and vacancy rates. Real estate investors must also be able to research zoning laws, building codes, and other regulations that may affect their investment.

4. Project Management

Real estate investors must be able to manage projects from start to finish. This includes hiring and managing contractors, obtaining permits, and overseeing renovations. Real estate investors must also be able to manage their time and resources effectively to ensure that projects are completed on time and within budget.

5. Risk Management

Real estate investors must be able to identify and manage risks. This includes assessing the potential risks of an investment and developing strategies to mitigate those risks. Real estate investors must also be able to adapt to changing market conditions and make adjustments to their investment strategy as needed.

How to Get Started in Real Estate Investing

If you’re interested in becoming a real estate investor, here are some steps to get started.

1. Educate Yourself

Learn as much as you can about real estate investing. Read books, take courses, and attend seminars. Consider getting a real estate license to gain a deeper understanding of the industry.

2. Set Goals

Determine what you want to achieve with real estate investing. Do you want to generate passive income, build wealth, or both? Set specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound (SMART) goals to guide your investment strategy.

3. Build a Team

Real estate investing is not a solo endeavor. Build a team of professionals who can help you succeed, including a real estate agent, attorney, accountant, and contractor.

4. Start Small

Consider starting with a small investment, such as a single-family home or a duplex. This will allow you to gain experience and build confidence before taking on larger projects.

5. Network

Connect with other real estate investors and professionals. Join real estate investment groups, attend networking events, and participate in online forums. Networking can help you learn from others’ experiences and find new opportunities.

Real estate investing can be a rewarding career path for those who are willing to put in the time and effort to learn the necessary skills. By understanding the different types of real estate, potential career paths, and essential skills, you can make informed decisions and build a successful real estate investment portfolio.
